content: Stream created by Rick Gleason.

I notice that you are using links to AsciiDoctor, yet I do not have this installed and am not using it.
To what extent is the AsciiDoctor documentation applicable? Is it interchangeable?

I see that https://docs.asciidoctor.org/asciidoc/latest/ is titled "AsciiDoc" so using that section would be ok.
The other resource is Antora https://docs.antora.org/antora/2.2/playbook/
I think the original asciidoc page is good for general information, but not formatting
The asciidoc format is well defined, so the asciidoctor docs should be applicable. Antora does not break the asciidoc syntax, it just adds more stuff on top of it.
BTW: It's easier, more light-weight to change the topic in the general discussion than to create full-blown streams.

Ok, I can Repurpose this Stream, to be AsciiDoc Format Questions.
Actually I can't change the Stream Text, just Use of Documentation.

I have found that asciidoc bullets and lists occupy too much vertical space for my needs.
Also there appears to be no line break code available Why? I could use that to create tighter lines.
I would like to make lists without bullet and without the wider line spacing. It is unnecessary for this purpose.

Does anyone have some suggestions?

  1. What is line break symbol?
    2.How to change the way lists work? Interline spacing primarily.

Found it

Inline line break syntax To preserve a line break in a paragraph, type a blank space followed by a plus sign ( + ) at the end of the line. This results in a visible line break (e.g., <br> ) following the line.
https://docs.asciidoctor.org/asciidoc/latest/blocks/hard-line-breaks/

Also listing style delimited listing block ____ (four of them)

I will remove this stream. Let's keep discussion about the manuals in the general stream for now. If need need be, we could create a "manuals" stream to contain all manual discussion. Topic is an easier, more light-weight and just better way to organize things within the stream
This topic was moved here from #Asciidoc vs AsciiDoctor? > AsciiDoc Format Questions by Alec Leamas.
